chunks.title: Course Overview
chunks.content:
Capstone Senior Design
Spring 2026 - College Station

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Capstone Senior Design. (1-6). Credit 3. Engineering design including identification of a problem; development, analysis and evaluation of alternative solutions; and recommendations for and, where possible, development of systems improvement tools; application of experience and training to provide a product or solution that helps company clients; balancing client needs with academic requirements.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
UCRT - University Required Oral Communication
This is a C course, so to pass the course students must pass the communications content. No matter how high the student's grade, if the communications component is not passed, the student fails the course.

chunks.title: Team Assignments
chunks.content: The instructional team will assign teams based on student input. Students who do not submit the course questionnaire by the established deadline will be assigned to a project without their input.

chunks.title: Team Meetings
chunks.content: Active engagement and participation are essential elements of the capstone project experience. Students are expected to fully contribute to all team activities, including meetings with sponsors, faculty advisors, and internal team discussions. These interactions are vital to the success of the project and provide opportunities to collaborate, problem-solve, and apply professional communication skills. Ensuring meaningful participation in these engagements supports both individual and team development throughout the course.

chunks.title: Additional Information
chunks.content:
The project sponsor is the client. This is the owner of the issue, problem, or opportunity central to the project.
Each team is required to select a faculty advisor. The faculty advisor is a Texas A&M faculty member who offers guidance on the project. Normally, the faculty advisor is selected by the team for their expertise in the area of their project.
The course faculty includes the lead instructor, communications instructors, and teaching assistants. The lead instructor is responsible for the capstone design program and for providing feedback to teams on the technical aspects of their projects. The course writing and communication instructors are responsible for executing the course's communication and writing components. Teaching assistants will also provide technical feedback as well as guidance on projects and presentations throughout the course (i.e. in advance).

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in ISEN 210, ISEN 330, ISEN 340, ISEN 350, ISEN 355 and ISEN 370; junior or senior classification.

This material Is: Required
No Textbook Needed

chunks.title: Additional Instructional Materials
chunks.content:

Additional phase-relevant resources will be provided via Canvas.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content: Grades will be a combination of team and individual grades as reflected in the chart below. Communications content must be passed with a C or higher to pass the class. Peer performance will be determined by an anonymous survey at the end of the course.

chunks.title: Course Grades
chunks.content:
| Team Grades | % of Final Grade | Grade Type |
|---|---|---|
| Project Charter | 5% | Technical |
| Phase Presentations 1-3 | 15% (5% ea) | Technical |
| Phase 4 Presentation | 10% | Technical |
| Project Showcase | 5% | Technical |
| Final Report | 20% | Technical |
| **Individual Grades** | | |
| Phase Presentations | 15% | Communication |
| Writing Assignments | 18% | Writing |
| Peer Performance | 12% | |

chunks.title: Technical Review Presentations
chunks.content: Technical review presentations are formal (prepared slides and remarks), graded events with all team members presenting and all team members participating. Every team member should contribute to answering questions at the end of the presentation. During technical review presentations, the lead instructor and teaching assistants will provide feedback on the technical aspects of the project, and the communication instructors will provide feedback on presentation design and delivery. Technical presentation slides must be uploaded to Canvas no later than 11:59 PM the day prior to the technical review session (per deliverables schedule).

chunks.title: Engineering Project Showcase Video
chunks.content: The Engineering Project Showcase Video is a mandatory participation event for all teams and all team members. Each team will prepare a video demonstrating their capstone project. Industry judges assess each team's work, and prize money is awarded to the top teams.

chunks.title: *** Individual Assignments
chunks.content: *** Individual Assignments

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Late work will not be accepted unless student rule 7 applies. Please notify the instructors in advance if possible if student rule 7 will apply to an assignment.

chunks.title: Class Meetings
chunks.content: Class attendance is required of all students for introductory (first two weeks) and phase kick-off sessions (as noted on schedule). For team presentation days, attendance is required for the entire class time for all team members in the specified group. Attendance on presentation days for the alternate group is allowed and encouraged.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
As previously noted, class attendance is required of all students for introductory (first two weeks) and phase kick-off sessions (as noted on schedule). For team presentation days, attendance is required for the entire class time for all team members in the specified group.
Lack of attendance/participation (including consistent tardiness) as noted by the instructors or peers (on the peer evaluation) will result in a reduction of the peer performance grade.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
With the emergence of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, the ways in which we define our creative processes continue to transform. AI generators are rapidly evolving from simple editing for grammatical errors and spelling mistakes (Grammarly, MS Word Spell Check) to sophisticated text production (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.), as well as image, computer code, and audio generation. The presence of such tools, however, does not replace our need to learn how to draft, revise, and reflect on texts, programs, drawings and how to exercise information literacy and personal responsibility in how we locate, evaluate, incorporate, and cite primary/ secondary sources. For example, the Association for Writing Across the Curriculum states the following: Writing to learn is an intellectual activity that is crucial to the cognitive and social development of learners and writers. This vital activity cannot be replaced by AI language generators (AWAC).
Engaging in the various aspects of creative pursuits (e.g., writing, coding, drawing) is critical to education in a broad sense. While AI technologies will continue shaping how we approach these creative tasks, the critical work of creativity relies on integrity, originality, and ethical conduct in regard to appropriate representation as an author or creator. Thus, submitting work with a significant percentage of AI-generated content, unless otherwise permitted, can be considered academic misconduct under Texas A&M University Student Rule 20. Students must therefore cite the use of Generative AI tools and document what they have contributed to an assignment.
You may use generative AI in this class with the following restrictions:
1. You may not divulge confidential client information to any AI model.
2. You must declare the AI model and its role in the work.
3. You must review and confirm all AI output.
